title: Optimization & Gradient Descent Theory
description: The theory and practice of gradient-based optimization: GD variants, momentum, adaptive methods, convergence analysis, learning rate schedules, and second-order methods.
details: Optimization is the engine that trains every ML model. This file covers gradient descent in depth: batch vs stochastic vs mini-batch, the challenges of non-convex optimization (local minima, saddle points, ill-conditioning), momentum and Nesterov accelerated gradient, adaptive methods (AdaGrad, RMSProp, Adam, AdamW), learning rate schedules (cosine annealing, warm restarts, cyclical LR), convergence analysis, and second-order methods (Newton, L-BFGS). Understanding why optimizers behave the way they do is a key differentiator in senior ML interviews.

sections.title: Gradient Descent Variants
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Gradient descent finds the minimum of a function by repeatedly taking steps in the opposite direction of the gradient — like a blindfolded hiker walking downhill. The three variants differ in how much data you use to estimate the gradient.

Three variants:

  • Batch Gradient Descent: w = w - α · (1/n) Σ ∇L(w, xᵢ, yᵢ). Uses all n samples per update. Deterministic — same gradient every epoch. Slow for large n (O(n) per step). Converges smoothly to minimum.
  • Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD): w = w - α · ∇L(w, xᵢ, yᵢ). Uses one random sample per update. Fast per step (O(1)). Gradient noise helps escape sharp local minima. Never converges exactly — oscillates around minimum. Needs learning rate decay.
  • Mini-Batch SGD: w = w - α · (1/m) Σ ∇L(w, xᵢ, yᵢ). Uses m samples (typically 32-512). Best of both worlds: efficient vectorized computation, lower gradient variance, converges faster than both Batch and pure SGD.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: 1M training examples. Batch GD: 1M gradient computations per step. SGD: 1 per step. Mini-batch (256): 256 per step, but 4x throughput due to vectorization. Result: mini-batch reaches target loss in 1/10 the wall-clock time.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.datasets import make_regression

X, y = make_regression(n_samples=1000, n_features=10, noise=0.1, random_state=42)
X = np.c_[np.ones((X.shape[0], 1)), X]  # add bias
m, n = X.shape
w = np.zeros((n, 1))
y = y.reshape(-1, 1)

# === Batch GD ===
def batch_gd(X, y, lr=0.01, epochs=1000):
    w = np.zeros((X.shape[1], 1))
    for epoch in range(epochs):
        grad = (2/m) * X.T @ (X @ w - y)
        w -= lr * grad
    return w

# === SGD (one sample at a time) ===
def sgd(X, y, lr=0.01, epochs=50):
    w = np.zeros((X.shape[1], 1))
    for epoch in range(epochs):
        for i in np.random.permutation(m):
            xi = X[i:i+1]
            yi = y[i:i+1]
            grad = 2 * xi.T @ (xi @ w - yi)
            w -= lr * grad
    return w

# === Mini-Batch GD ===
def minibatch_gd(X, y, lr=0.01, epochs=100, batch_size=32):
    w = np.zeros((X.shape[1], 1))
    for epoch in range(epochs):
        indices = np.random.permutation(m)
        for start in range(0, m, batch_size):
            batch = indices[start:start + batch_size]
            Xi, yi = X[batch], y[batch]
            grad = (2/len(batch)) * Xi.T @ (Xi @ w - yi)
            w -= lr * grad
    return w

w_batch = batch_gd(X, y)
w_sgd = sgd(X, y)
w_mini = minibatch_gd(X, y)

sections.title: Momentum & Nesterov Accelerated Gradient
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Momentum adds a velocity term that accumulates gradients — like a ball rolling downhill: it picks up speed in consistent directions and dampens oscillations in directions that keep reversing.

NAG goes one step further: it looks ahead (compute gradient at the predicted next position) before taking the step, giving the optimizer foresight.

Formulas:

  • Momentum: vt = β · vt-1 + α · ∇L(wt); wt+1 = wt - vt. β is typically 0.9. Velocity accumulates gradients over time, with decay.
  • NAG (Nesterov): vt = β · vt-1 + α · ∇L(wt - β · vt-1); wt+1 = wt - vt. Gradient is evaluated at the lookahead position (w - βv), not the current position.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: On a typical deep network training on CIFAR-10, SGD reaches 85% accuracy in 200 epochs. Momentum (β=0.9) reaches 85% in 120 epochs. NAG reaches 85% in 105 epochs. NAG converges faster and to a slightly better optimum.

Momentum intuition by analogy: Without momentum, SGD at a plateau wanders randomly. With momentum, velocity builds in whatever direction was consistently downhill — the optimizer 'commits' to a direction and maintains it, cutting through plateaus faster.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np

# === Gradient Descent with Momentum ===
def momentum_gd(X, y, lr=0.01, beta=0.9, epochs=100):
    m, n = X.shape
    w = np.zeros((n, 1))
    v = np.zeros((n, 1))
    
    for epoch in range(epochs):
        grad = (2/m) * X.T @ (X @ w - y)
        v = beta * v + lr * grad
        w -= v
    return w

# === Nesterov Accelerated Gradient ===
def nesterov_gd(X, y, lr=0.01, beta=0.9, epochs=100):
    m, n = X.shape
    w = np.zeros((n, 1))
    v = np.zeros((n, 1))
    
    for epoch in range(epochs):
        # Lookahead step
        w_lookahead = w - beta * v
        grad = (2/m) * X.T @ (X @ w_lookahead - y)
        v = beta * v + lr * grad
        w -= v
    return w

# Compare convergence
w_plain = batch_gd(X, y, lr=0.01, epochs=500)
w_momentum = momentum_gd(X, y, lr=0.01, epochs=500)
w_nesterov = nesterov_gd(X, y, lr=0.01, epochs=500)

# Momentum typically reaches lower loss in fewer epochs

sections.title: Adaptive Optimizers (AdaGrad, RMSProp, Adam, AdamW)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: All parameters share the same learning rate in SGD. Adaptive methods give each parameter its own learning rate — frequently updated features get smaller steps, rare features get larger steps.

Adaptive methods:

  • AdaGrad: Accumulates squared gradients in a cache: ct = ct-1 + gt². Learning rate per param: α / (√ct + ε). Problem: cache grows monotonically, learning rate shrinks to zero over time. Good for sparse features (NLP).
  • RMSProp: Uses exponential moving average instead of sum: ct = βct-1 + (1-β)gt². Fixes AdaGrad's shrinking issue. Learning rate stays active throughout training.
  • Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation): Combines momentum (first moment) + RMSProp (second moment): mt = β₁mt-1 + (1-β₁)gt; vt = β₂vt-1 + (1-β₂)gt²; bias-corrected; w -= α · m̂ / (√v̂ + ε). Default β₁=0.9, β₂=0.999, ε=1e-8.
  • AdamW: Fixes a bug in Adam: weight decay should be decoupled from gradient-based updates. w -= α(m̂/(√v̂+ε) + λw). Improves generalization vs Adam.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Training BERT: AdamW with cosine schedule, β₁=0.9, β₂=0.999, ε=1e-6, weight decay 0.01. SGD would take 10x longer and perform worse due to the large parameter count and sparse gradients in the embedding layer.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np

# === Adam from scratch ===
def adam(X, y, lr=0.001, beta1=0.9, beta2=0.999, eps=1e-8, epochs=100):
    m, n = X.shape
    w = np.zeros((n, 1))
    mt = np.zeros((n, 1))
    vt = np.zeros((n, 1))
    
    for t in range(1, epochs + 1):
        grad = (2/m) * X.T @ (X @ w - y)
        
        # Biased moments
        mt = beta1 * mt + (1 - beta1) * grad
        vt = beta2 * vt + (1 - beta2) * (grad ** 2)
        
        # Bias correction (important early in training)
        mt_hat = mt / (1 - beta1 ** t)
        vt_hat = vt / (1 - beta2 ** t)
        
        w -= lr * mt_hat / (np.sqrt(vt_hat) + eps)
    return w

# === Using PyTorch optimizers ===
# optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3, weight_decay=0.01)

# === Learning rate comparison ===
# SGD with momentum
# optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.1, momentum=0.9)
#
# Adam (default for transformers)
# optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-4, betas=(0.9, 0.999))
#
# AdamW (better generalization)
# optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-4, weight_decay=0.01)

sections.title: Learning Rate Schedules
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: The learning rate is the most important hyperparameter. A good schedule starts high (fast progress) and decays (fine-grained convergence). The right schedule can mean the difference between a model that converges and one that diverges.

Common schedules:

  • Step Decay: Drop LR by γ (e.g., 0.1) every K epochs. Simple, effective. Most common: reduce by 10x at epoch 150, 250 of 300 total.
  • Exponential Decay: αt = α0 · e-kt. Smooth decay, never reaches zero.
  • Cosine Annealing: αt = αmin + ½(α0 - αmin)(1 + cos(t/T · π)). Smooth decay following cosine curve. Used in most modern vision models and Transformers.
  • Cosine with Warm Restarts: Cosine cycles: decay to 0, then jump back to α0 and restart. Each cycle is shorter (SGDR: Stochastic Gradient Descent with Warm Restarts). Helps escape local minima.
  • Linear Warmup + Decay: Linearly increase LR from 0 to αmax over first N steps, then cosine/linear decay to 0. Standard for large-scale training (BERT, GPT). Critical for preventing divergence in first steps.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: BERT pre-training: 1M steps, warmup = 10K steps (linear from 0 to 1e-4), then linear decay to 0. Without warmup: loss spikes to NaN in first 100 steps. With warmup: stable training throughout.

sections.title: Convergence Analysis & Challenges
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Optimization is hard because the loss surface is not a nice convex bowl — it's a rugged landscape with mountains, valleys, and flat regions.

Key challenges:

  • Local Minima: Historically feared, now understood to be rare in high dimensions. Most local minima of large neural nets are within 1-2% of the global minimum and generalize similarly. The real problem isn't local minima — it's saddle points.
  • Saddle Points: Points where gradient is zero but loss curves down in some directions and up in others. In high-D, most zero-gradient points are saddles. GD slows to a crawl because gradient is tiny near the saddle. Momentum and adaptive methods help by accumulating gradient signal.
  • Ill-Conditioning: The Hessian has a wide range of eigenvalues. In steep directions, GD overshoots. In shallow directions, GD creeps. Condition number = λmax / λmin. High condition number → slow convergence. Adaptive methods help by normalizing per-parameter LR.
  • Plateaus: Regions where gradient is near zero for all directions but loss is far from minimum. Often caused by saturating activations (sigmoid, tanh) or poorly initialized weights.

Convergence rates:

  • Convex functions: GD converges at O(1/T). Nesterov accelerates to O(1/T²).
  • Strongly convex: GD converges at O(e-μT) — linear/geometric convergence.
  • Non-convex (neural nets): No guarantee of convergence to global minimum. In practice, SGD finds a
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np

# === Saddle point example: f(x,y) = x² - y² ===
# The point (0,0) is a saddle point (min in x, max in y)

def saddle_function(w):
    x, y = w[0], w[1]
    return x**2 - y**2

def saddle_gradient(w):
    x, y = w[0], w[1]
    return np.array([[2*x], [-2*y]])

# Gradient descent gets stuck near saddle point
w = np.array([[0.1], [0.1]])
lr = 0.01
for _ in range(100):
    w -= lr * saddle_gradient(w)
print(f'GD near saddle: {w.ravel()}')  # very close to (0,0)

# Momentum escapes the saddle
w = np.array([[0.1], [0.1]])
v = np.zeros((2, 1))
beta = 0.9
for _ in range(100):
    grad = saddle_gradient(w)
    v = beta * v + lr * grad
    w -= v
print(f'Momentum escapes: {w.ravel()}')  # moves along y direction

# === Condition number example ===
# f(x,y) = 1000*x² + y² (ill-conditioned: cond = 1000)
def ill_conditioned_grad(w):
    x, y = w[0], w[1]
    return np.array([[2000*x], [2*y]])

w = np.array([[1.0], [1.0]])
for _ in range(50):
    grad = ill_conditioned_grad(w)
    w -= 0.01 * grad
print(f'Ill-conditioned GD: {w.ravel()}')  # y converges fast, x is still far from 0

sections.title: Second-Order Methods (Newton, L-BFGS)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: First-order methods use the slope (gradient) to descend. Second-order methods also use the curvature (Hessian) to know how far to step — they 'see' both the direction and the shape of the loss surface.

Second-order methods:

  • Newton's Method: wt+1 = wt - Ht⁻¹ · ∇L(wt). Uses the Hessian (matrix of second derivatives) to scale the gradient. In a quadratic bowl, converges in ONE step. Problem: H is n×n, O(n³) to invert — infeasible for neural nets with millions of parameters.
  • Quasi-Newton (BFGS): Builds an approximation of H⁻¹ using only gradient information from past steps. No O(n³) inversion needed. Still O(n²) memory to store the approximation — too large for deep learning.
  • L-BFGS (Limited Memory BFGS): Stores only the last m gradient differences (typically m=10-100) instead of the full approximate Hessian. Memory is O(mn) instead of O(n²). Works well for deterministic optimization with batch GD. Used for logistic regression, SVM training. Not suitable for stochastic (mini-batch) settings.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: For a model with 10M parameters: L-BFGS stores ~200 vectors (20 recent grad diffs) × 10M = 2B floats ≈ 8GB memory. Adam stores 2 moments × 10M = 20M floats ≈ 80MB. Memory cost makes second-order methods impractical for deep learning.
sections.codeBlock:
from scipy.optimize import minimize
import numpy as np

# === L-BFGS via scipy (used for small models) ===
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# sklearn uses L-BFGS by default for logistic regression
model = LogisticRegression(solver='lbfgs', max_iter=500)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# === Manual L-BFGS with scipy ===
def loss(w, X, y):
    y_pred = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-X @ w))
    return -np.mean(y * np.log(y_pred + 1e-15) + (1-y) * np.log(1 - y_pred + 1e-15))

def grad(w, X, y):
    y_pred = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-X @ w))
    return (1/len(X)) * X.T @ (y_pred - y)

result = minimize(
    loss, w0, args=(X, y),
    method='L-BFGS-B',
    jac=grad,
    options={'maxiter': 100, 'disp': True}
)
print(f'L-BFGS converged in {result.nit} iterations')

# === When to use second-order vs first-order ===
# L-BFGS: small datasets, deterministic loss, logistic regression, SVMs
# Adam/SGD: deep learning, mini-batch training, large-scale problems

# === Natural Gradient (second-order for probabilistic models) ===
# Uses Fisher Information Matrix instead of Hessian
# Invariant to reparameterization — elegant but expensive
# Used in: TRPO, ACKTR (reinforcement learning)

sections.title: Practical Optimization Workflow
sections.description:
[Practical Workflow] Choosing the right optimizer: There's no single best optimizer — it depends on your model architecture, data scale, and computational budget.

Hyperparameter tuning order:

  • 1. Learning rate: Most important. Try values [1, 0.1, 0.01, 0.001, 0.0001]. Use LR range test (increase LR exponentially over a few epochs, pick the one with steepest descent).
  • 2. Batch size: Larger batch → stabler gradients, but worse generalization (sharp minima). Typical: 32, 64, 128, 256. Rule: double batch, double LR to keep gradient variance constant.
  • 3. Momentum/β₁: 0.9 is default for both momentum and Adam. 0.95 for very smooth loss surfaces. 0.5 for noisy gradients.
  • 4. Weight decay: For AdamW, try [0.1, 0.01, 0.001]. For SGD + momentum, use L2 penalty in loss (functionally similar).

[Concrete Numbers] LR range test: Start LR at 1e-7, increase by 1% per mini-batch. Plot loss vs LR. Loss will decrease, then start increasing. Pick LR at the bottom of the valley, ~1 order of magnitude before it diverges. This single trick eliminates days of manual LR tuning.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import numpy as np

# === LR Range Test (learning rate finder) ===
def lr_range_test(model, loader, loss_fn, start_lr=1e-7, end_lr=10, num_iter=100):
    """Increase LR exponentially, return loss vs LR"""
    optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=start_lr)
    lrs, losses = [], []
    
    for i, (inputs, targets) in enumerate(loader):
        if i >= num_iter:
            break
        
        # Compute current LR
        lr = start_lr * (end_lr / start_lr) ** (i / num_iter)
        for param_group in optimizer.param_groups:
            param_group['lr'] = lr
        
        # Forward + backward
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        outputs = model(inputs)
        loss = loss_fn(outputs, targets)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        
        lrs.append(lr)
        losses.append(loss.item())
    
    return np.array(lrs), np.array(losses)

# === Recommended PyTorch setup for different scenarios ===

# Tabular / small model: SGD + Momentum + Cosine
# optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.1, momentum=0.9, weight_decay=1e-4)
# scheduler = torch.optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=100)

# Large model / Transformer: AdamW + Warmup + Cosine
# optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-4, weight_decay=0.01)
# scheduler = torch.optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=100)

# Sparse features: Adam (higher epsilon for stability)
# optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3, eps=1e-6)

# === Key rule: Linear Scaling Rule ===
# When doubling batch size, double learning rate
# Keeps gradient variance approximately constant
# Example: batch=64, lr=0.1 → batch=128, lr=0.2
